doc_description: This document provides a comprehensive guide on optimizing protein intake for individuals using GLP-1 medications, featuring a tailored meal plan, detailed recipes, preparation tips, and a grocery list to support healthy eating and weight loss.

structure.title: Protein Balance for GLP -1s
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of protein balance in relation to GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) medications. It likely includes a meal plan designed to optimize protein intake while considering the effects of GLP-1s on metabolism and appetite regulation. The document appears to be part of a larger guide from the Mayo Clinic Diet, focusing on dietary strategies to support individuals using GLP-1 therapies.
structure.text:
Protein Balance for GLP -1s
Meal Plan
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 1 of 23

nodes.title: Welcome to the Protein Balance for GLP -1s Meal Plan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a meal plan tailored for individuals taking GLP-1 medications who experience low appetite. Key points include the importance of consuming small, frequent meals to avoid skipping, and the flexibility to not achieve perfect adherence to food group targets initially. The plan emphasizes easy-to-consume, lower volume meals such as smoothies, soups, and stews that provide necessary nutrients for healthy weight loss. It also encourages batch cooking to simplify meal preparation and ensure availability of healthy options. Additionally, it suggests incorporating vegetable and fruit-based snacks, with the option to skip them in the early weeks if appetite is low, while highlighting the benefits of these snacks for hydration and nutrient intake once appetite improves.
nodes.text:
Welcome to the Protein Balance for GLP -1s Meal Plan
This simple meal plan is specifically designed for members taking GLP-1s with low appetites. This higher-
protein meal plan helps preserve muscle tissue and includes easy-to-consume, lower volume meals along 
with tips and guidance to achieve healthy weight loss and ensure your nutritional needs are met.
Eat small, frequent meals
It's best to avoid skipping meals, so if you can't eat an entire meal in one go, split it into two smaller meals 
eaten two to three hours apart. For example, save your fruit and nuts from breakfast for a mid-morning snack, 
or eat half your sandwich for lunch and the second half as a mid-afternoon snack. Try to eat dinner early, 
giving your body at least two hours to digest the food before going to bed.
Don't strive for perfection
In the first few weeks of starting weight-loss medication, it's OK if you can't reach your daily food group 
targets shown in your Meal Plan and Food & Exercise Tracker, just try to come as close to the targets as you 
can.
Enjoy easy-to-consume meals
The meals included in this meal plan are generally smaller in volume so you don't have to battle through big 
meals, but they still deliver the nutrients required for healthy weight loss. You can expect plenty easy-to-
consume meals like smoothies, soups, and stews. 
Embrace batch cooking
Each week, you will batch cook a few freezable meals. This helps to reduce the amount of cooking required in 
the first few weeks when eating feels like a chore. Batch cooking enables you to have healthy meals available 
to defrost and reheat each week. Stock up on a few small to medium-sized food containers so you can freeze 
individual portions.
Enjoy your vegetable & fruit based snacks
If you're struggling to eat the snack in your meal plan, it's OK to skip these for the first few weeks. Although, 
the smoothies, soups, and ice pops provide protein and are a great way to pack in more nutrients while also 
keeping you hydrated. Once your appetite returns, you can go back to eating unlimited vegetables and fruits 
as a way to manage your hunger.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 2 of 23

nodes.title: Meal Plan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a detailed meal plan for a week, including breakfast, lunch, dinner, and snacks for each day from April 17 to April 23. It emphasizes the use of leftovers to minimize food waste and includes specific recipes such as Vanilla overnight oats, Butternut & lentil soup, Beef bolognese with polenta, and Loaded green smoothie. Additionally, it provides a checklist for meal preparation, including tips for ingredient shopping, meal swapping, and preparation steps for various dishes. The document also suggests ways to enhance meals with herbs and spices and offers advice on selecting protein powder. Overall, it serves as a comprehensive guide for meal planning and preparation for the week.

Loaded green
smoothie© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 3 of 23Here’s your checklist to help you prepare for Week 1.
1.Swap meals to suit your preferences. If you don’t like a particular
meal, go to your Meal Plan  and click the “Swap” button to select a
different recipe.
2.Shop for all the ingredients. Review the Grocery List  at the end of
this PDF or on the website. Adjust the list to cater for your family
and needs. Check off items you already have.
3.Make high-protein milk. Boost the protein of your milk by adding
non-fat dry milk powder at a ratio of 1 oz milk powder to every 1 cup
milk. Use this high-protein milk throughout the week.
4.Prepare Vanilla overnight oats with banana & peanut butter*.  Divide
the overnight oats mixture between four separate containers with
lids (or eight containers if your appetite is low and you're splitting
breakfast into two smaller meals.) Store in the fridge.
5.Freeze a banana.  Peel a banana, cut it in half, and freeze the two
halves ahead of time for the Berry smoothie with peanut butter
toast*.
6.Prepare Loaded green smoothie*. Pour into four containers and
place in the fridge for up to 24 hours or in the freezer for up to 3
months.
7.Butternut & lentil soup*. Makes 4 servings. You'll eat three servings
this week and freeze one serving for Week 2.
8.Beef bolognese with polenta*.  Makes 6 servings. You'll eat four
servings this week and freeze two servings for Week 6.
9.Sheet-pan chicken, broccoli & sweet potato*.  Makes 3 servings.
You will eat all three servings across three dinners this week.
10.Freeze leftover ingredients. Store any leftover ingredients like bread, wraps or cheese in the freezer to use
in future weeks.
Extra tips:  
1.Add your own flavor.  Fresh and dried herbs and spices, along with aromatics like garlic, ginger and chili are
all free foods, so feel free to add these or use what you have in your pantry to boost flavor.
2.Protein powder.  Look for an option that contains at least 15 grams of protein per serving, and has a short
ingredients list and limited added sugars — ideally 100% whey, soy, or pea protein.
*Refers to recipes in the meal plan as specified. Your meal plan may differ if you have swapped meals.Prep steps and tips for this week
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 4 of 23

nodes.title: Prep steps and tips for this week
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides a checklist for preparing meals for Week 1 of a meal plan. Key points include:

1. Customizing meals by swapping recipes to match personal preferences.
2. Shopping for ingredients using a provided grocery list, with adjustments for family needs.
3. Making high-protein milk by adding non-fat dry milk powder.
4. Preparing Vanilla overnight oats and storing them in containers for easy access.
5. Freezing bananas for use in smoothies.
6. Preparing and storing Loaded green smoothies for later consumption.
7. Cooking Butternut & lentil soup and Beef bolognese with polenta, with instructions on portioning and freezing leftovers for future weeks.
8. Preparing Sheet-pan chicken, broccoli, and sweet potato for dinners.
9. Freezing leftover ingredients for future use.
10. Additional tips for enhancing flavor with herbs and spices, and selecting a suitable protein powder.

The document emphasizes meal preparation, storage, and customization to fit individual dietary needs.

nodes.title: Breakfast meals
nodes.summary: The partial document provides a recipe for "Vanilla Overnight Oats with Banana & Peanut Butter," detailing the ingredients and method for preparation. It serves four and includes old-fashioned oats, milk, non-fat dry milk, Greek yogurt, vanilla extract, bananas, and reduced sugar peanut butter. The preparation involves mixing the ingredients and refrigerating them overnight. Each serving is topped with additional yogurt, banana, and peanut butter. Nutritional information is also provided, indicating the servings contain specific amounts of fruit, carbohydrates, protein, fats, and total calories.
nodes.text:
Breakfast meals
Vanilla overnight oats with banana & peanut butter
Vanilla overnight oats with banana & peanut butter, makes 4 servings
Old fashioned (rolled) oats, 1 cup(s) (2.8oz)
1% milk, 1 cup(s) (8.3fl oz)
Non-fat dry milk, instant, with added vitamin A and D, 1 oz,
(dissolved into the milk) (1oz)
Non-fat plain Greek yogurt, 4 cup(s) (2lb)
Vanilla extract, 1 tsp(s) (0.1oz)
Banana, 2 large, sliced (9.6oz)
Peanut butter, reduced sugar & salt, 2 tbsp(s) (1.1oz)Method
Add the oats, milk, dry milk, 1 cup of yogurt and vanilla into a bowl. Stir until 
well combined. Divide the oats mixture between four separate jars or 
containers with lids. Store in the fridge overnight.
Each morning, grab one serving and top with ¾ cup of yogurt, half a banana 
and ½ tablespoon of peanut butter. Enjoy!
Each serving of ‘Vanilla overnight oats with banana & peanut butter’ 
provides: 1.0 Fruit, 1.1 Carbohydrates, 1.7 Protein/Dairy, 1.1 Fats, 374 calories, 
32.8g protein.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 5 of 23

nodes.title: Lunch meals
nodes.summary: The partial document provides a recipe for Butternut & Lentil Soup, which serves four. It includes a list of ingredients such as extra virgin olive oil, leek, ginger, curry powder, butternut squash, carrots, lentils, and vegetable liquid stock. The method outlines the cooking process, starting with sautéing the leek, ginger, and curry powder, followed by adding the remaining ingredients and simmering until tender. The soup is then blended until smooth and served. Additionally, there is a tip for storing leftovers and nutritional information per serving, detailing the content of vegetables, carbohydrates, protein, fats, and calories.
nodes.text:
Lunch meals
Butternut & lentil soup
Butternut & lentil soup, makes 4 servings
Extra virgin olive oil, 4 tsp(s) (0.6oz)
Leek, 1 leek(s), finely chopped (4.4oz)
Ginger, 1 tsp(s), finely grated (0.1oz)
Curry powder, 1 tbsp(s) (0.2oz)
Butternut squash, raw, 35 oz, peeled, seeded, chopped
(2.2lb)
Carrots, 2 medium, peeled and chopped (4.3oz)
Lentils, dry, 1 cup(s) (6.8oz)
Vegetable liquid stock, 4 cup(s) (1qt)Method
Heat the oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the leek, ginger and 
curry powder, cook stirring for 3 to 4 minutes or until leek is soft. Add the 
butternut squash, carrot and lentils, stir to coat in the curry mixture.
Add the stock and 1 cup of water. Increase heat to high, cover and bring to the 
boil. Reduce heat to medium. Boil gently, covered, stirring occasionally for 10 
minutes or until the butternut is tender. Remove from the heat, stand covered 
for 10 minutes.
Carefully use a stick blender to blend until smooth. Divide the soup between 
serving bowls. Serve.
TIP: If you're cooking for 1, cool and freeze in single servings or place in the fridge 
for up to 4 days.
Each serving of ‘Butternut & lentil soup’ provides: 0.9 Vegetables,    
1.6 Carbohydrates, 1.5 Protein/Dairy, 0.9 Fats, 363 calories, 17.4g protein.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 9 of 23

nodes.title: Dinner meals
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines two meal recipes: Beef Bolognese with Polenta and a Yogurt & Cucumber snack. 

1. **Beef Bolognese with Polenta**: This recipe serves six and includes ingredients such as extra virgin olive oil, extra lean ground beef, garlic, white onion, canned diced tomatoes, black pepper, whole-grain cornmeal, and unsalted butter. The method involves cooking the beef with garlic and onion, adding tomatoes, and simmering until thick. Polenta is prepared by whisking cornmeal into boiling water and simmering until thickened. Each serving provides nutritional information, including vegetables, carbohydrates, protein, fats, and calories.

2. **Yogurt & Cucumber**: This simple snack consists of non-fat Greek yogurt and cucumber sticks, making one serving. It provides nutritional details similar to the main dish, highlighting its vegetable and protein content along with calorie information.

Overall, the document emphasizes meal preparation, portioning, and nutritional values for healthy eating.
nodes.text:
Dinner meals
Beef bolognese with polenta
Beef bolognese with polenta, makes 6 servings
Extra virgin olive oil, 4 tsp(s) (0.6oz)
Ground beef, extra lean, 1 lb(s) (1lb)
Garlic, minced, 2 tbsp(s) (0.6oz)
White onion, chopped, 2 cup(s) (11.3oz)
Tomatoes, canned, diced, no salt added, 2 x 14½ oz can(s)
(1.8lb)
Black pepper, 4 dash(es)
Cornmeal, whole-grain, 1 cup(s) (4.3oz)
Unsalted butter, 1 tbsp(s) (½oz)Method
Divide into six equal servings. Enjoy one serving for dinner. Store three servings in 
airtight containers in the fridge for later in the week. Store two remaining servings in 
the freezer for a future week.
Beef bolognese with polenta instructions
Heat the oil in a large saucepan over medium-high heat.
Add the beef and cook, stirring occasionally, for 4 to 5 minutes until browned. Add 
garlic and onion stirring for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the tomatoes and season with black pepper. Bring to a boil and then reduce 
the heat and simmer until thick, for about 5 minutes.
In a medium saucepan, bring 4 cups of water to a boil. Slowly whisk in the cornmeal 
to ensure no clumping. Reduce heat, cover and simmer, stirring occasionally until 
thickened, about 10 to 15 minutes. Remove from heat, stir in the butter.
Divide the polenta into six equal servings and top each serving with bolognese. Serve.
Each serving of ‘Beef bolognese with polenta’ provides: 2.2 Vegetables, 
1.1 Carbohydrates, 0.9 Protein/Dairy, 1.0 Fats, 276 calories, 20.2g protein.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 13 of 23
Yogurt & cucumber
Non-fat Greek yogurt, any flavor, 1 x 5.3 oz container(s) (5.3oz)
Cucumber, 1 small, cut into sticks (5.6oz)
Method
Enjoy the tub of yogurt and cucumber sticks as snacks during the day.
Makes 1 serving. Each serving provides: 0.9 Vegetables, 1.1 Protein/Dairy,
 140 calories, 13.6g protein.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 19 of 23Snack meals

nodes.title: Snack meals
nodes.summary: The partial document provides a simple snack recipe featuring non-fat Greek yogurt and cucumber sticks. It specifies the ingredients needed, including one container of yogurt and one small cucumber, and outlines the method of enjoying them as a snack. The document also includes nutritional information for the serving, detailing the vegetable and protein/dairy content, as well as the total calorie count.
nodes.text:
Yogurt & cucumber
Non-fat Greek yogurt, any flavor, 1 x 5.3 oz container(s) (5.3oz)
Cucumber, 1 small, cut into sticks (5.6oz)
Method
Enjoy the tub of yogurt and cucumber sticks as snacks during the day.
Makes 1 serving. Each serving provides: 0.9 Vegetables, 1.1 Protein/Dairy,
 140 calories, 13.6g protein.
© 2024 Mayo Clinic Diet Page 19 of 23Snack meals

nodes.title: Grocery List
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a grocery list organized into three main categories: Fruits & Vegetables, Pantry Items, and Bread & Bakery. 

In the Fruits & Vegetables section, a variety of fresh produce is listed, including apples, avocados, spinach, bananas, broccoli, butternut squash, carrots, celery, cucumbers, fresh herbs, garlic, ginger, leeks, lemons, pears, raspberries, sweet potatoes, tomatoes, watermelon, and onions, with specific weights provided for each item.

The Pantry Items section includes a range of non-perishable food items such as canned salmon, whole-grain cornmeal, various spices (black pepper, curry powder), cooking oils (extra virgin olive oil), dry lentils, condiments (light mayo, Sriracha, sweet chili sauce), canned tomatoes, nutritional yeast, oats, peanut butter, and vegetable stock, along with instant non-fat dry milk.

The Bread & Bakery section lists whole-grain bread and whole-wheat tortillas, specifying the quantity and weight of each. 

Overall, the document emphasizes a focus on healthy, whole foods and a balanced diet.
